---
title: "Synthesis"

execute:
  freeze: false
  cache: false

title-block-style: none

knitr:
  opts_chunk:
    dev: "ragg_png"
---

::: {.content-visible when-format="pdf"}
\clearpage
\providecommand{\chaptermark}[1]{}
\ifcsname c@chapter\endcsname
\setcounter{chapter}{6}
\setcounter{section}{0}
\addcontentsline{toc}{chapter}{\protect\numberline{6}Synthesis}
\chaptermark{Synthesis}
\fi
\providecommand{\thechapter}{6}
\thispagestyle{empty}
\begin{tikzpicture}[remember picture, overlay]
  \node[inner sep=0pt] at ([yshift=-3.5cm]current page.center)
    {\includegraphics[width=\paperwidth,height=\paperheight]{images/ch6_cover.jpg}};
  \fill[black]
    (current page.north west) rectangle ([yshift=-7cm]current page.north east);
  \node[color=thesissand, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{13}{16}\selectfont\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-1.5cm]current page.north west)
    {Chapter \thechapter};
  \node[color=white, text width=0.85\paperwidth, align=left,
        font=\fontsize{22}{28}\selectfont\bfseries\sffamily, anchor=west]
    at ([xshift=1.5cm, yshift=-4cm]current page.north west)
    {Synthesis};
\end{tikzpicture}
\null
\clearpage
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="docx"}
![](images/ch6_cover.jpg){fig-align="center" width=15cm}

*What do these findings mean for the future of kōura habitat restoration?*

{{< pagebreak >}}
:::

::: {.content-visible when-format="html"}
# Synthesis {.unnumbered}
:::

## Stones pile field trial Lake Ōkaro
In this thesis several stone piles were assambled in Lake Rotoiti but before those were deployed the construction process by using a trailer and bringing the rocks to the lake was tested in Lake Ōkaro. Lake Ōkaro is one of the smaller Rotorura Te Arawa lakes and is also volcanic with an area of 30.19 ha, a max and mean depth of 18 m and 12.5 m. Waterquality in the lake has been very poor with tropic state flipping between supertrophic and eutrophic and often algae blooms. To improve water quality in the lake in 2005 a wetland has been constructed to remove nitrogens and sediments off the main inflows into the lake. In lake phosphorous treatment by alim dosings are execute 2 times a year in the lake. Kōura have not been present in Lake Ōkaro in monitorings from 1981 to 2007 [@Parkyn2007]. Monitoring in 2016 and 2017 still found no kōura in the lake even as waterquality had been improved [@Kusabs2017]. waterquality now should be far enough restored that kōura should be able to survive in the lake but natural recolonosation has not happened yet therefore reintroduction might be possible. Lake Ōkaro is stocked with trout for angling and as a preadaor of kōura, extra refuge and habitat by stone piles would be good to trial in this lake. The lake has no stone or rocky substrates therefore the construction of two stone piles would provide refuge for the introduced kōura in the lake. 

Construction of the stone piles was executed by all parties involved in the lake with people from Te Arawa Lake Trust, Ngāti Tahu - Ngāti Whaoa, Te Mana o Ngāti Rangitihi Trust, Bay of Plenty Regional Council and The University of Waikato. Stones were sourced from Rainbow Quarry located on Rainbow Mountain three km from the lake. Stones used had a size range of 70-210 mm mean 120 mm and a total of 5 m3 was was divided over two stone piles on the north east shore of the lake. Stone piles were placed in a T-shape with the top of each 5 m long horizontal with the shore at 3.4 and 6 m of the shore at 1 m depth. To follow dissolved oxygen and temperature devalopment of the stone piles and how they compare to the rest of the lake on 11 September 2024 three HOBO U26-001 Dissolved Oxygen Data Loggers, were placed in the lake for a period of six months. Two loggers were buried inside each of the stone piles and the third logger was placed outside of the stone piles as control. In October 2024 twenty kōura sourced from within the catchment were reintroduction on the stone piles to start a new population of kōura in the lake. 

In the early summer of 2024 the lake stratified rapidly with oxygen levels dropping quike and at December 10th oxygen levels reaching less than 3 mg L⁻¹ at water surface level for multiple days (@fig-okaro-combined). The HOBO loggers at the shoreline and in the stone piles showed that inside the DO levels dropped slightly faster than compared to the control site and that the daily average of DO was lower than outside of the stone piles (@fig-okaro-combined). This is expected as the sensors buried inside the stones were able to get less mixed water and as close to periphyton the oxygen levels will deplet more during the night. For koura it is expected that they will hide only on the outside of the stone piles and emerge at night to forange. Due to the anoxic event in the lake, trout were dying and also for koura it was expected that they might not have survived this event. 

![Daily mean water temperature (top) and dissolved oxygen (bottom) at Ōkaro stone pile, control sites, and the lake buoy < 1m. Period: 2024-09-11 to 2025-02-27.](06_synthesis/outputs/fig-okaro-combined.png){#fig-okaro-combined}

## Deep stone piles Lake Rotoiti
In chapter 5 of this thesis stone piles were constructed on the shoreline of Lake Rotoiti as they were deployed by hand with a trailer, similar way as was trialed in Lake Ōkaro. Due to the limited range and mean depth of deployment the question remained if the stone piles would be effective deeper in the lake and if stone piles would be able to suppress invasive machrophyte beds. In November 2025 therefore four stone piles were constructed at various depths. Three of the stone piles were constructed perpendicular to the shoreline. The first one was placed underneed a jetty at around 1 m depth. The second one was placed in the middle of ainvasive machrophyte bed infront of the jetty around 3 m depth. The third one was placed at the end of the machrophytes at a depth of 7 till 9 m depth. To prevent the stones from sinking into the sediment, wooden slaps were first installed wherafter the stones were placed ontop keeping the wooden slaps inplace. The fourth stone pile was constructed on a bedrock shelf 120 m away from the other stone piles. As these stones were placed ontop of bedrock no wooden slaps were needed. Construction was executed by a boat with a winch lowering bags of stones to the preferd location guided by divers. 

Monitoring was executed two times before construction and one time after construction using spotlighting where after sunset the shoreline and any available area under the jetty was observed with a torch and kōura counted and their size estimated. The deeper locations were monitored using a ROV (FIFISH V-EVO) at night footage was made of the location before and after stone pile construction and kōura numbers were counted. Kōura CPUE was calculated over the time spent filming and the amount of kōura counted. 

The first pre monitoring (30/06/2025, water temp 13 °C) resulted in two kōura seitings while spotlighting and two by the ROV, second pre monitoring (26/08/2025, water temp 11 °C) showed zero kōura with spotlighting but seven with the ROV. The monitoring after construction executed on 1/05/2026 (water temp 16 °C) revealt ten kōura by spotlighting around at the underneed the jetty and the ROV counted eleven kōura on the deeper stone piles. Most kōura were seen at the border of the stone piles where they met with the sandy substrate this was expected as visual it was hard to look inside the stone piles and kōura were observed when emerged and started foraging. The stone pile ontop of the machrophyte bed showed a complete block of machrofyte growed but at the sides the machrophyes were present densely making kōura observations difficult. 

The trial of placing stone piles at various depths revealt that stone piles do quickly attract kōura from the surroundings and are able to suppress invasive macrophytes. By placing stone piles at several depths kōura can shift to hiding places most suited.
